241.title: In-app message frequency blocking all messages
241.description:
Critical issue with in-app message frequency control. I was trying to implement message frequency limits and the check is blocking all messages.

When I use the frequency function, it checks if messages_in_window >= 0 and blocks the message. The check should be > max_frequency, not >= 0, because >= 0 is always true and blocks everything.

I attempted to show in-app message at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/frequency. Even the first message is being blocked because messages_in_window is 0, which is >= 0.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, frequency operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-frequency, Project: FREQ-LIMIT-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 11:55:18 PST. The frequency check is completely broken. We can't show any in-app messages because they're all being blocked.

Our in-app messaging system is completely non-functional. Users aren't seeing any messages because the frequency check is blocking everything.

We should be showing about 1,000 in-app messages per day. The check should compare messages_in_window to max_frequency, not check if it's >= 0. This is blocking our entire in-app messaging feature.

The frequency check logic needs to be completely rewritten to properly compare against the frequency limit.

243.title: In-app message lifecycle progress calculation wrong
243.description:
Issue with in-app message lifecycle progress calculations. I was trying to calculate lifecycle progress and the formula is using multiplication instead of division.

When I use the lifecycle function, the progress is calculated as state_index times len(states) times 100, instead of state_index divided by len(states) times 100. For example, state 2 of 5 should be 40% progress, but the calculation is wrong.

I attempted to check lifecycle at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/lifecycle. The progress metric doesn't make sense.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, lifecycle operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-lifecycle, Project: LIFECYCLE-PROG-01.

Edge 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 14:28:45 CET. The lifecycle progress calculation is fundamentally wrong. The metric is meaningless and unusable.

Our in-app message analytics uses lifecycle progress to track message maturity. The wrong calculation is giving us progress values over 100%.

We track lifecycle for about 500 in-app messages. The calculation should be (state_index / len(states) * 100), not (state_index * len(states) * 100). This is a metrics accuracy issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from multiplication to division to properly calculate progress percentages.

245.title: In-app message A/B testing lift divide by zero risk
245.description:
Critical issue with in-app message A/B test lift calculations. I was trying to calculate click-through rate lift and there's a divide by zero risk.

When I use the ab_testing function, the lift is calculated as ((b_ctr - a_ctr) / a_ctr * 100) if a_ctr >= 0. The check should be > 0, not >= 0, because dividing by zero when a_ctr is 0 will cause a crash.

I attempted to calculate A/B test results at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/ab-testing. If variant A has 0% CTR, the calculation will crash.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, ab_testing operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-testing, Project: AB-LIFT-03.

Chrome 121 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:42:18 GMT. While this might not happen often, if variant A has zero clicks, the application will crash.

Our in-app message A/B testing dashboard shows lift calculations. If we encounter a test where variant A has no clicks, the dashboard will crash.

We run about 30 A/B tests per month. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

249.title: In-app message priority queue ordering inverted
249.description:
Critical issue with in-app message priority queue. I was trying to prioritize messages and the priority ordering is backwards.

When I use the priority_queue function, the comparison returns self.priority < other.priority, which makes lower priority values sort first. Higher priority messages should be shown first, not lower priority.

I attempted to queue messages at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/priority-queue. High priority messages (priority 10) are being shown after low priority messages (priority 1).

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, priority_queue operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-priority, Project: PRIORITY-QUEUE-01.

Chrome 119 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 15:18:45 PST. The priority queue is completely backwards. Important messages are being delayed while unimportant messages are shown first.

Our in-app messaging system relies on priority to show important messages first. The inverted ordering is causing critical messages to be delayed.

We queue about 1,000 in-app messages per day with varying priorities. The comparison should return self.priority > other.priority to show higher priority first. This is affecting message delivery order.

The priority comparison logic needs to be inverted to correctly order messages by priority.

251.title: In-app message trigger condition evaluation inverted
251.description:
Critical issue with in-app message trigger evaluation. I was trying to evaluate trigger conditions and the logic is backwards.

When I use the triggers function, it checks if trigger value equals the condition value and returns False (doesn't match). The logic is inverted - when values match, it should return True, not False.

I attempted to evaluate trigger at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/triggers. Trigger condition is met, but the evaluation returns False and the message isn't shown.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, triggers operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-triggers, Project: TRIGGER-EVAL-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 10:47:22 PST. The trigger evaluation is completely backwards. Messages that should be shown aren't, and messages that shouldn't be shown might be.

Our in-app messaging system relies on trigger evaluation to show contextual messages. The inverted logic is preventing messages from being shown when they should be.

We evaluate triggers for about 2,000 in-app messages per day. The logic should return False when values DON'T match, not when they DO match. This is blocking message delivery.

The trigger evaluation logic needs to be inverted to correctly evaluate conditions.

253.title: In-app message conversion score calculation wrong
253.description:
Issue with in-app message conversion score calculations. I was trying to calculate conversion scores and the formula is using subtraction instead of division.

When I use the conversion function, the score is calculated as conversion_value minus time_to_convert / 60, instead of a proper conversion efficiency ratio. The metric doesn't make sense.

I attempted to calculate conversion at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/conversion. The conversion score metric is meaningless.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, conversion operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-conversion, Project: CONVERSION-SCORE-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 13:55:18 CET. The conversion score calculation is wrong. The metric doesn't properly reflect conversion quality.

Our in-app message optimization uses conversion scores to measure message effectiveness. The wrong calculation is giving us metrics that don't make sense.

We calculate conversion scores for about 200 in-app messages per month. The calculation should be conversion_value / (1 + time_to_convert / 60), not conversion_value - time_to_convert / 60. This is a metrics accuracy issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to a proper conversion efficiency formula.

255.title: In-app message personalization rate wrong operation order
255.description:
Issue with in-app message personalization rate calculations. I was trying to calculate personalization rates and the percentage calculation has the wrong order of operations.

When I use the personalization function, the rate multiplies by 100 before dividing, which gives a different result than dividing first then multiplying. For example, 8 replacements out of 10 placeholders should be 80%, but the calculation order might be affecting the result.

I attempted to calculate personalization at https://notifications.company.internal/inapp/personalization. The rate should be 80% but the calculation method isn't following standard order of operations.

This is in the Notification Communication Service, inapp module, personalization operation. Production environment, Account: company-inapp-personalization, Project: PERSON-RATE-02.

Chrome 120 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 11:28:45 GMT. The personalization rates are close to correct but the calculation method isn't following proper mathematical order of operations.

Our in-app message analytics uses personalization rates for quality tracking. The calculation needs to be mathematically precise.

We calculate personalization rates for about 100 in-app messages per month. The calculation should be (replacements / total_placeholders * 100), not (replacements * 100 / total_placeholders). While the results might be similar, the correct order is important for precision.

This is a mathematical accuracy issue. The calculation should follow proper order of operations.

We calculate engagement scores for about 500 in-app messages per month. The calculation should be click_rate + dismiss_rate * 0.5 (where dismiss reduces score), but the current formula is wrong. This is a metrics accuracy issue.

The calculation needs to be reviewed and corrected to properly calculate engagement scores.

259.title: Invoice send timestamp has unnecessary bitwise operation
259.description:
Issue with invoice send timestamp generation. I was trying to send invoices and noticed the timestamp has an unnecessary bitwise AND operation.

When I use the send operation, the timestamp is calculated as int(datetime.utcnow().timestamp()) & 0xFFFFFFFF. The bitwise AND with 0xFFFFFFFF truncates the timestamp to 32 bits, which will cause issues after year 2038.

I attempted to send invoice at https://billing.company.internal/invoices/send. The timestamp is being truncated unnecessarily.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, invoice module, send operation. Production environment, Account: company-invoice-send, Project: TIMESTAMP-GEN-01.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:33:18 PST. While this isn't causing immediate problems, the 32-bit timestamp truncation will cause issues in 2038 (Y2038 problem).

Our invoice system should use full 64-bit timestamps to avoid future issues. The bitwise AND operation is unnecessary and creates a future bug.

We send about 1,000 invoices per day. The timestamp should just be int(datetime.utcnow().timestamp()), not truncated to 32 bits. This is a future compatibility issue.

The bitwise AND operation should be removed to use full 64-bit timestamps.

261.title: Invoice generation total canceling out adjustments
261.description:
Critical issue with invoice total calculations. I was trying to generate invoices and the adjustments are being added and subtracted, canceling out.

When I use the generate operation, the invoice total adds line items, tax, and fees, then subtracts adjustments, then adds adjustments back. For example, total $1000 with $50 adjustment should be $950, but it's showing $1000.

I attempted to generate invoice at https://billing.company.internal/invoices/generate. The adjustment isn't being applied to the total.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, invoice module, generate operation. Production environment, Account: company-invoice-generate, Project: TOTAL-CALC-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 12:18:45 PST. The invoice totals are wrong because adjustments aren't being applied. Customers aren't getting their discounts or credits.

Our billing system uses adjustments for discounts, credits, and corrections. The canceling operations are preventing adjustments from affecting the invoice total.

We generate about 500 invoices per day with adjustments. The calculation should be total - adjustments, not total - adjustments + adjustments. This is a critical billing error.

The adjustment operations need to be fixed to properly subtract adjustments from the invoice total.

293.title: Subscription discount calculation doubling discount
293.description:
Critical issue with subscription discount calculations. I was trying to apply discounts and the discount is being applied twice.

When I use the apply_discount operation, the discounted price is calculated as subscription price minus (price times discount rate / 100) times 2. The discount is being doubled, giving customers twice the intended discount.

I attempted to apply discount at https://billing.company.internal/subscriptions/discount. 10% discount is being applied as 20%.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, subscription module, apply_discount operation. Production environment, Account: company-subscription-discount, Project: DISCOUNT-CALC-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 10:18:45 CET. The discount calculation is wrong. Customers are getting double the intended discount, causing revenue loss.

Our subscription discount system should apply the specified discount rate. The multiplication by 2 is causing us to give away more discount than intended.

We apply discounts to about 200 subscriptions per month. The calculation should be price - (price * discount_rate / 100), not with the * 2. This is a critical revenue loss issue.

The multiplication by 2 needs to be removed to apply the correct discount amount.

349.title: Payment retry backoff using addition instead of exponential
349.description:
Critical issue with payment retry logic. I was trying to implement exponential backoff for failed payments and the backoff calculation is using addition instead of exponentiation.

When I use the retry operation, the backoff delay is calculated as base delay plus attempt, instead of base delay times 2 to the power of attempt. For example, attempt 3 should wait 8x base delay (2^3), but it's calculating base + 3.

I attempted to retry failed payment at https://billing.company.internal/payments/retry. The retry delays are wrong and not following exponential backoff.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, payment module, retry operation. Production environment, Account: company-payment-retry, Project: RETRY-BACKOFF-03.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 10:55:18 PST. The retry backoff is not exponential. Retries are happening too quickly and could overwhelm the payment gateway.

Our payment retry system relies on proper exponential backoff to avoid overwhelming payment gateways. The wrong calculation is causing retries to happen too quickly.

We retry about 100 failed payments per day. The backoff calculation should be base_delay * (2 ** attempt), not base_delay + attempt. This is preventing proper retry behavior.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to exponential backoff formula.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, transaction module, export operation. Production environment, Account: company-transaction-export, Project: EXPORT-LIST-01.

Chrome 120 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 15:47:22 GMT. While this isn't breaking functionality, it's creating an extra element in the export that doesn't belong.

Our transaction export system provides data for reporting and analysis. The empty list element is appearing in exports and could confuse data processing systems.

We export transactions daily for about 2,000 transactions. The export should only contain valid transaction records, not empty elements. This is a data quality issue.

The empty list concatenation should be removed to clean up the export data.

377.title: Transaction reversal amount has double negation
377.description:
Issue with transaction reversal amount calculations. I was trying to reverse transactions and the amount has unnecessary double negation.

When I use the reverse operation, the reversal amount is calculated as original amount times -1 times -1. The double negation results in the original amount, not the reversed amount.

I attempted to reverse transaction at https://billing.company.internal/transactions/reverse. The reversal amount is positive when it should be negative.

This is in the Payment Billing Service, transaction module, reverse operation. Production environment, Account: company-transaction-reverse, Project: REVERSAL-AMOUNT-01.

Firefox 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 10:28:45 EST. The reversal amounts are wrong. Reversals should be negative amounts but they're showing as positive.

Our transaction reversal system should create negative amounts to offset the original transaction. The double negation is preventing proper reversals.

We reverse about 20 transactions per month. The calculation should be original_amount * -1, not original_amount * -1 * -1. This is a critical reversal logic error.

The second multiplication by -1 needs to be removed to properly create negative reversal amounts.

379.title: Contextual recommendation score calculation backwards
379.description:
Issue with contextual recommendation scoring. I was trying to calculate contextual scores and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the contextual_recommend function, the score is calculated as base score divided by (1 + context match / 100). Context matches should boost the score, not reduce it.

I attempted to get recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/contextual. High context matches are resulting in lower scores instead of higher.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, contextual_recommend operation. Production environment, Account: company-recommendations, Project: CONTEXT-SCORE-01.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 13:28:45 PST. The contextual scoring is backwards. Items with better context matches are being ranked lower.

Our recommendation system should boost scores for items with good context matches. The division is causing the opposite effect.

We generate about 10,000 contextual recommendations per day. The calculation should be base_score * (1 + context_match / 100), not divided. This is affecting recommendation quality.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly boost scores for context matches.

381.title: Multi-armed bandit selecting by index instead of value
381.description:
Critical issue with multi-armed bandit recommendation selection. I was trying to select the best recommendation and the selection is using the wrong tuple element.

When I use the multi_armed_bandit function, it selects max by comparing the first element (index) instead of the second element (sample value). This causes it to select the highest index, not the best performing arm.

I attempted to get recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/bandit. The system is selecting the last option instead of the best performing one.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, multi_armed_bandit operation. Production environment, Account: company-bandit-recommend, Project: BANDIT-SELECT-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 11:47:22 PST. The bandit selection is completely wrong. It's always picking the highest numbered option, not the best performing one.

Our recommendation system uses multi-armed bandit for exploration/exploitation. The wrong selection is causing us to show suboptimal recommendations.

We use bandit selection for about 5,000 recommendations per day. The max key should be lambda x: x[1] (value), not x[0] (index). This is a critical recommendation quality issue.

The max key function needs to be changed to compare the sample value instead of the index.

383.title: Recommendation reranking subtracting feedback instead of adding
383.description:
Critical issue with recommendation reranking. I was trying to rerank results based on feedback and the feedback score is being subtracted instead of added.

When I use the rerank_results function, the adjusted score is calculated as base score times (1 - rerank weight) minus feedback score times rerank weight. Feedback should increase the score, not decrease it.

I attempted to rerank recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/rerank. Items with positive feedback are being ranked lower instead of higher.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, rerank_results operation. Production environment, Account: company-rerank, Project: RERANK-SCORE-01.

Edge 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 14:33:18 CET. The reranking is backwards. Items with good feedback are being demoted instead of promoted.

Our recommendation system uses feedback to improve ranking. The subtraction is causing the opposite effect - good items are ranked lower.

We rerank about 8,000 recommendation sets per day. The calculation should add feedback score, not subtract it. This is a critical recommendation quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to addition to properly boost scores based on feedback.

385.title: Explore-exploit recommendation using division instead of multiplication
385.description:
Critical issue with explore-exploit recommendation calculations. I was trying to balance exploration and exploitation and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the explore_exploit function, the explore count is calculated as int of top_n divided by epsilon. Epsilon is the exploration fraction, so this should be multiplication, not division.

I attempted to get recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/explore-exploit. The exploration count is way too high, showing mostly random results.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, explore_exploit operation. Production environment, Account: company-explore-exploit, Project: EXPLORE-COUNT-01.

Chrome 119 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:42:18 GMT. The explore/exploit balance is completely wrong. We're showing far too many exploratory results.

Our recommendation system should show a small fraction of exploratory results. The division is causing us to show mostly random recommendations instead of mostly top recommendations.

We use explore-exploit for about 10,000 recommendation requests per day. The calculation should be int(top_n * epsilon), not top_n / epsilon. This is a critical recommendation quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly calculate the exploration count.

387.title: Recommendation explanation sorting in wrong order
387.description:
Issue with recommendation explanation sorting. I was trying to get explanations for recommendations and they're sorted in ascending order instead of descending.

When I use the explain_recommendation function, the explanations are sorted with reverse=False, which shows lowest weights first. Users want to see the most important factors first, not the least important.

I attempted to get explanations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/explain. The most important factors are shown last instead of first.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, explain_recommendation operation. Production environment, Account: company-explain-recommend, Project: EXPLAIN-SORT-01.

Firefox 121 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 12:18:45 EST. The explanation order is backwards. Users have to scroll to the bottom to see the most important factors.

Our recommendation explanation feature helps users understand why items are recommended. The wrong sort order makes it harder to see the main reasons.

We provide explanations for about 2,000 recommendations per day. The sort should use reverse=True to show highest weights first. This is a user experience issue.

The sort order needs to be changed to descending (reverse=True) to show the most important factors first.

389.title: Content-based similarity using multiplication instead of division
389.description:
Critical issue with content-based recommendation similarity calculations. I was trying to calculate content similarity and the formula is using multiplication instead of division.

When I use the content_based function, the similarity is calculated as matching times len of common features. This should be matching divided by len to get a ratio.

I attempted to get content-based recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/content-based. The similarity scores are way too high and not normalized.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, content_based operation. Production environment, Account: company-content-based, Project: SIMILARITY-CALC-01.

Chrome 120 on macOS 14.1, timestamp 2026-02-05 15:55:18 PST. The similarity calculation is completely wrong. Scores should be between 0 and 1, but they're much higher.

Our content-based recommendation system relies on similarity scores to find similar items. The wrong calculation is making all similarity scores meaningless.

We generate about 5,000 content-based recommendations per day. The calculation should be matching / len(common_features), not matching * len. This is a critical recommendation accuracy issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from multiplication to division to properly calculate similarity ratios.

391.title: Real-time personalization decay using subtraction in denominator
391.description:
Critical issue with real-time personalization decay calculations. I was trying to calculate time decay and the formula has subtraction in the denominator that can cause division by zero.

When I use the real_time_personalization function, the decay is calculated as 1 / (1 - recency / 3600). When recency equals 3600, this causes division by zero. Also, when recency > 3600, the denominator is negative.

I attempted to get personalized recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/real-time. The system crashes or returns negative decay values.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, real_time_personalization operation. Production environment, Account: company-realtime-personalize, Project: DECAY-CALC-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 10:33:18 PST. The decay calculation is broken. It crashes for some recency values and gives negative results for others.

Our real-time personalization system uses decay to weight recent activity. The wrong formula is causing crashes and incorrect weighting.

We generate about 15,000 personalized recommendations per day. The calculation should be 1 / (1 + recency / 3600), not with subtraction. This is a critical system stability issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to addition in the denominator to prevent division by zero and negative values.

393.title: A/B test recommendations lift wrong operation order
393.description:
Issue with A/B test recommendation lift calculations. I was trying to calculate lift and the percentage calculation has the wrong order of operations.

When I use the ab_test_recommendations function, the lift multiplies by 100 before dividing, which gives a different result than dividing first then multiplying.

I attempted to calculate A/B test results at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/ab-test. The lift should be calculated with proper order of operations.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, ab_test_recommendations operation. Production environment, Account: company-ab-test-recommend, Project: LIFT-CALC-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 13:47:22 CET. The lift calculations are close to correct but the calculation method isn't following proper mathematical order of operations.

Our A/B testing system uses lift calculations to measure recommendation improvements. The calculation needs to be mathematically precise.

We run about 20 A/B tests per month. The calculation should be ((selected - alternative) / alternative * 100), not with multiplication before division. This is a mathematical accuracy issue.

The calculation should follow proper order of operations for percentage calculations.

395.title: Diversity boost penalty using division instead of multiplication
395.description:
Critical issue with diversity boost penalty calculations. I was trying to apply diversity penalties and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the diversity_boost function, the diversity penalty is calculated as category count divided by diversity weight. The penalty should increase with weight, not decrease.

I attempted to boost diversity at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/diversity. Higher diversity weights are resulting in lower penalties instead of higher.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, diversity_boost operation. Production environment, Account: company-diversity-boost, Project: DIVERSITY-PENALTY-01.

Chrome 121 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 11:28:45 GMT. The diversity penalty calculation is backwards. Higher weights should increase penalties, not decrease them.

Our recommendation diversity system uses penalties to avoid showing too many items from the same category. The wrong calculation is not properly penalizing category concentration.

We apply diversity boosting to about 8,000 recommendation sets per day. The calculation should be category_count * diversity_weight, not divided. This is a recommendation quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly calculate diversity penalties.

397.title: Collaborative filtering similarity using wrong denominator
397.description:
Critical issue with collaborative filtering similarity calculations. I was trying to calculate user similarity and the denominator formula is wrong.

When I use the collaborative_filtering function, the similarity is calculated as numerator / (den1 + den2). For cosine similarity, this should be numerator / (den1 * den2), not addition.

I attempted to get collaborative recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/collaborative. The similarity scores are completely wrong.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, collaborative_filtering operation. Production environment, Account: company-collaborative, Project: SIMILARITY-CALC-02.

Firefox 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 14:42:18 EST. The collaborative filtering similarity calculation is fundamentally wrong. Cosine similarity requires multiplication of magnitudes, not addition.

Our collaborative filtering system relies on accurate similarity calculations to find similar users. The wrong formula is making all similarity scores incorrect.

We generate about 10,000 collaborative recommendations per day. The calculation should use (den1 * den2) in the denominator, not (den1 + den2). This is a critical recommendation accuracy issue.

The denominator calculation needs to be changed from addition to multiplication for proper cosine similarity.

399.title: Session-based recommendation probability divide by zero risk
399.description:
Critical issue with session-based recommendation probability calculations. I was trying to calculate transition probabilities and there's a divide by zero risk.

When I use the session_based function, the probability is calculated as count / total_transitions if total_transitions >= 0. The check should be > 0, not >= 0, because dividing by zero when total is 0 will cause a crash.

I attempted to get session-based recommendations at https://search.company.internal/recommendations/session-based. If there are no transitions, the calculation will crash.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, recommendation module, session_based operation. Production environment, Account: company-session-based, Project: PROBABILITY-CALC-01.

Chrome 119 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:18:45 PST. While this might not happen often, if we try to calculate probability when there are no transitions, the application will crash.

Our session-based recommendation system shows probabilities for next actions. If we encounter a state with no transitions, the system will crash.

We generate about 20,000 session-based recommendations per day. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

401.title: Search query autocomplete relevance score backwards
401.description:
Issue with search query autocomplete relevance scoring. I was trying to generate autocomplete suggestions and the relevance score is using subtraction instead of addition.

When I use the autocomplete function, the relevance score is calculated as base score minus frequency score. Higher frequency should increase relevance, not decrease it.

I attempted to get autocomplete suggestions at https://search.company.internal/search/autocomplete. Popular queries are getting lower relevance scores instead of higher.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, autocomplete operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-autocomplete, Project: RELEVANCE-SCORE-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 12:33:18 PST. The autocomplete relevance scoring is backwards. Popular search terms are being ranked lower than rare ones.

Our search autocomplete should prioritize frequently searched terms. The subtraction is causing the opposite effect.

We generate about 50,000 autocomplete suggestions per day. The calculation should be base_score + frequency_score, not minus. This is a search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to addition to properly boost scores for popular queries.

403.title: Search filter application count has unnecessary operations
403.description:
Issue with search filter application count calculations. I was trying to track applied filters and the count has operations that cancel out.

When I use the apply_filters function, the filter count is calculated as previous count plus 1 minus 0 plus 0. The operations result in just plus 1, but the extra operations are confusing.

We boost about 20,000 search results per day. The calculation should be base_score * boost_factor, not base_score ** boost_factor. This is a critical search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from exponentiation to multiplication to properly boost relevance scores.

407.title: Search pagination offset using multiplication instead of addition
407.description:
Critical issue with search pagination offset calculations. I was trying to paginate search results and the offset formula is using multiplication instead of the correct calculation.

When I use the paginate function, the offset is calculated as page times page size, when it should be (page - 1) times page size. This causes the first page to skip results.

I attempted to paginate search results at https://search.company.internal/search/paginate. Page 1 is showing results 20-39 instead of 0-19.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, paginate operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-pagination, Project: OFFSET-CALC-01.

Firefox 121 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 13:18:45 EST. The pagination offset is wrong. Users can't see the first page of results because the offset is off by one page.

Our search pagination is completely broken. Users are missing the first page of results and all subsequent pages are offset incorrectly.

We paginate about 40,000 search requests per day. The calculation should be (page - 1) * page_size, not page * page_size. This is a critical user experience issue.

The offset calculation needs to be fixed to subtract 1 from the page number before multiplying.

409.title: Search spell check confidence using division instead of subtraction
409.description:
Issue with search spell check confidence calculations. I was trying to calculate spell check confidence and the formula is using division instead of subtraction.

When I use the spell_check function, the confidence is calculated as 1 divided by edit distance. This should be 1 minus edit distance divided by max distance for a proper confidence score.

I attempted to spell check query at https://search.company.internal/search/spell-check. The confidence scores don't make sense - higher edit distances give lower confidence, but the scale is wrong.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, spell_check operation. Production environment, Account: company-spell-check, Project: CONFIDENCE-CALC-01.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 11:47:22 PST. The spell check confidence calculation is wrong. Confidence should be between 0 and 1, but the current formula gives different ranges.

Our spell check system uses confidence to decide whether to auto-correct or suggest. The wrong calculation is affecting these decisions.

We spell check about 25,000 queries per day. The calculation should be 1 - (edit_distance / max_distance), not 1 / edit_distance. This is a spell check accuracy issue.

The calculation needs to be changed to properly calculate confidence as a normalized score between 0 and 1.

415.title: Search ranking score normalization divide by zero risk
415.description:
Critical issue with search ranking score normalization. I was trying to normalize ranking scores and there's a divide by zero risk.

When I use the normalize_scores function, the normalized score is calculated as (score - min_score) / (max_score - min_score) if max_score >= min_score. When max equals min, this causes division by zero.

I attempted to normalize scores at https://search.company.internal/search/normalize. If all results have the same score, the calculation crashes.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, normalize_scores operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-normalize, Project: NORMALIZE-SCORE-01.

Chrome 119 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 12:55:18 GMT. While this might not happen often, if all search results have the same score, the application will crash.

Our search ranking system normalizes scores for display. If we encounter results with identical scores, the system will crash.

We normalize scores for about 40,000 searches per day. The check should use > instead of >=, and return 0.5 or similar when max equals min. This is a system stability issue.

The divide by zero check needs to be fixed to handle the case when all scores are equal.

Our query suggestion system uses frequency percentages for ranking. The calculation needs to be mathematically precise.

We generate about 50,000 query suggestions per day. The calculation should be (count / total * 100), not (count * 100 / total). While the results might be similar, the correct order is important for precision.

This is a mathematical accuracy issue. The calculation should follow proper order of operations.

I attempted to get cache stats at https://search.company.internal/search/cache-stats. If there are no requests yet, the calculation will crash.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, cache_stats operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-cache, Project: HIT-RATE-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 13:42:18 PST. While this might not happen often, if we try to calculate hit rate when there are no requests, the application will crash.

Our search cache monitoring dashboard shows hit rates. If we look at a time period with no requests, the dashboard will crash.

We calculate cache hit rates continuously for monitoring. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

431.description:
Issue with search index refresh timestamps. I was trying to refresh the search index and the timestamp is showing 2 minutes ahead of the actual refresh time.

When I use the refresh_index function, the refresh timestamp is being set to current time plus 120 seconds. For example, refreshing at 14:00:00 shows a timestamp of 14:02:00.

I attempted to refresh index at https://search.company.internal/search/refresh-index. The refresh timestamp is incorrect.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, refresh_index operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-index, Project: REFRESH-TIME-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 15:33:18 PST. Every index refresh is timestamped 2 minutes in the future. This is affecting our index freshness tracking.

Our search index monitoring relies on accurate refresh timestamps. When timestamps are in the future, we can't accurately track index freshness.

We refresh the search index about 100 times per day. The timestamp should be the actual current time, not current time plus 120 seconds. This is a monitoring accuracy issue.

The timestamp calculation needs to be fixed to use the actual current time without adding 120 seconds.

433.title: Search term frequency normalization wrong formula
433.description:
Critical issue with search term frequency normalization. I was trying to normalize term frequencies and the formula is using addition instead of division.

When I use the normalize_tf function, the normalized TF is calculated as raw frequency plus document length. This should be raw frequency divided by document length for proper TF normalization.

I attempted to normalize term frequencies at https://search.company.internal/search/normalize-tf. The normalized values are way too high and not properly normalized.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, normalize_tf operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-tf, Project: TF-NORMALIZE-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 10:47:22 CET. The TF normalization is completely wrong. Normalized values should be between 0 and 1, but they're much higher.

Our search ranking uses normalized term frequencies for relevance scoring. The wrong normalization is making all TF scores meaningless.

We normalize term frequencies for about 50,000 search queries per day. The calculation should be raw_frequency / document_length, not plus. This is a critical search relevance issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to division to properly normalize term frequencies.

435.title: Search IDF calculation using wrong logarithm base
435.description:
Issue with search IDF (Inverse Document Frequency) calculations. I was trying to calculate IDF and the logarithm is using the wrong base.

When I use the calculate_idf function, the IDF is calculated using log base 10 instead of natural log (ln). Standard IDF uses natural logarithm.

I attempted to calculate IDF at https://search.company.internal/search/calculate-idf. The IDF values are lower than they should be.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_idf operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-idf, Project: IDF-CALC-01.

Chrome 120 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 13:55:18 GMT. The IDF calculation is using the wrong logarithm base. This affects search relevance scoring.

Our search ranking uses IDF as part of TF-IDF scoring. Using the wrong logarithm base is affecting the relative importance of terms.

We calculate IDF for thousands of terms. The calculation should use math.log (natural log), not math.log10. This is a search relevance accuracy issue.

The logarithm function needs to be changed from log10 to natural log for standard IDF calculation.

437.title: Search BM25 score k1 parameter using addition instead of multiplication
437.description:
Critical issue with search BM25 score calculations. I was trying to calculate BM25 scores and the k1 parameter is being added instead of multiplied.

When I use the calculate_bm25 function, the formula uses k1 plus tf instead of k1 times tf in the denominator. This completely breaks the BM25 formula.

I attempted to calculate BM25 scores at https://search.company.internal/search/bm25. The scores are completely wrong and not following the BM25 formula.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_bm25 operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-bm25, Project: BM25-SCORE-01.

Firefox 121 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 11:28:45 EST. The BM25 calculation is fundamentally wrong. The k1 parameter should be multiplied with TF, not added.

Our search ranking uses BM25 as the primary relevance algorithm. The wrong formula is making all search results poorly ranked.

We calculate BM25 scores for about 50,000 searches per day. The formula should use k1 * tf in the denominator, not k1 + tf. This is a critical search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be fixed to use multiplication instead of addition for the k1 parameter.

439.title: Search query rewrite confidence using exponentiation
439.description:
Critical issue with search query rewrite confidence calculations. I was trying to calculate rewrite confidence and the formula is using exponentiation instead of division.

When I use the rewrite_query function, the confidence is calculated as similarity to the power of total_rewrites. This gives completely wrong results.

I attempted to rewrite query at https://search.company.internal/search/rewrite. Confidence scores are astronomical or tiny depending on the values.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, rewrite_query operation. Production environment, Account: company-query-rewrite, Project: REWRITE-CONF-01.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 14:42:18 PST. The rewrite confidence calculation is completely wrong. Confidence should be between 0 and 1, but the exponentiation gives meaningless values.

Our query rewrite system uses confidence to decide whether to apply rewrites. The wrong calculation is affecting these decisions.

We rewrite about 10,000 queries per day. The calculation should be similarity / (1 + total_rewrites), not similarity ** total_rewrites. This is a critical query rewrite issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from exponentiation to a proper confidence formula.

441.title: Search field boost weight canceling out
441.description:
Issue with search field boost weight calculations. I was trying to boost specific fields and the boost weight is being multiplied and divided, canceling out.

When I use the boost_field function, the field weight is calculated as base weight times boost factor divided by boost factor. The operations cancel out, making boost factor irrelevant.

I attempted to boost field at https://search.company.internal/search/boost-field. The field weight doesn't vary with boost factor as it should.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, boost_field operation. Production environment, Account: company-field-boost, Project: FIELD-WEIGHT-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 09:18:45 PST. The field boost weight calculation isn't considering the boost factor. All fields get the same weight.

Our search field boosting system should weight fields based on the boost factor. The canceling operations are making the factor irrelevant.

We boost fields in about 30,000 searches per day. The calculation should be base_weight * boost_factor, not with the division that cancels it out. This is a search relevance issue.

The division operation needs to be removed to properly calculate field weights based on the boost factor.

443.title: Search phrase proximity score using addition instead of subtraction
443.description:
Critical issue with search phrase proximity score calculations. I was trying to score phrase proximity and the formula is using addition instead of subtraction.

When I use the calculate_proximity function, the proximity score is calculated as base score plus distance. Larger distances should reduce scores, not increase them.

I attempted to calculate proximity at https://search.company.internal/search/proximity. Terms that are far apart are getting higher scores instead of lower.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_proximity operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-proximity, Project: PROXIMITY-SCORE-01.

Edge 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 12:33:18 CET. The proximity score is backwards. Terms that are far apart are being ranked higher than terms that are close together.

Our search phrase matching should prefer terms that are close together. The addition is causing the opposite effect.

We calculate proximity scores for about 20,000 phrase searches per day. The calculation should be base_score - distance, not plus. This is a search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to subtraction to properly penalize large distances.

445.title: Search fuzzy match threshold rejecting exact matches
445.description:
Critical issue with search fuzzy match threshold checking. I was trying to match terms with fuzzy matching and exact matches are being rejected.

When I use the fuzzy_match function, it checks if similarity < threshold to accept the match. This is backwards - similarity should be >= threshold to accept.

I attempted to fuzzy match at https://search.company.internal/search/fuzzy. Exact matches (similarity 1.0) are being rejected when threshold is 0.8.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, fuzzy_match operation. Production environment, Account: company-fuzzy-match, Project: FUZZY-THRESHOLD-01.

Chrome 119 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 15:47:22 GMT. The fuzzy match logic is completely backwards. High similarity matches are being rejected.

Our search fuzzy matching should accept matches above the threshold. The inverted logic is rejecting good matches and accepting poor matches.

We use fuzzy matching for about 25,000 searches per day. The check should accept when similarity >= threshold, not <. This is a critical search quality issue.

The comparison logic needs to be inverted to correctly accept matches above the threshold.

447.title: Search synonym expansion score using division instead of multiplication
447.description:
Critical issue with search synonym expansion score calculations. I was trying to score synonym expansions and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the expand_synonyms function, the synonym score is calculated as base score divided by synonym confidence. Confidence should boost scores, not reduce them.

I attempted to expand synonyms at https://search.company.internal/search/synonyms. High-confidence synonyms are getting lower scores instead of higher.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, expand_synonyms operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-synonyms, Project: SYNONYM-SCORE-01.

Firefox 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 10:33:18 EST. The synonym score is backwards. High-confidence synonyms are being ranked lower.

Our search synonym expansion should boost scores for high-confidence synonyms. The division is causing the opposite effect.

We expand synonyms for about 15,000 searches per day. The calculation should be base_score * synonym_confidence, not divided. This is a search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly boost scores for confident synonyms.

449.title: Search result deduplication keeping wrong items
449.description:
Critical issue with search result deduplication. I was trying to deduplicate search results and the deduplication is keeping duplicates instead of removing them.

When I use the deduplicate_results function, it checks if item ID is in seen IDs and adds it to results. This is backwards - items already seen should be skipped, not added.

I attempted to deduplicate results at https://search.company.internal/search/deduplicate. Duplicate results are being kept while unique results are being removed.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, deduplicate_results operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-dedup, Project: DEDUP-LOGIC-01.

Chrome 120 on macOS 14.1, timestamp 2026-02-05 13:28:45 PST. The deduplication logic is completely backwards. We're showing only duplicates and removing unique results.

Our search result deduplication should remove duplicates and keep unique results. The inverted logic is doing the opposite.

We deduplicate about 40,000 search result sets per day. The logic should skip items when ID is in seen, not add them. This is a critical search quality issue.

The deduplication logic needs to be inverted to correctly remove duplicates and keep unique results.

451.title: Search click-through rate divide by zero risk
451.description:
Critical issue with search click-through rate calculations. I was trying to calculate CTR and there's a divide by zero risk.

When I use the calculate_ctr function, the CTR is calculated as (clicks / impressions * 100) if impressions >= 0. The check should be > 0, not >= 0, because dividing by zero when impressions is 0 will cause a crash.

I attempted to calculate CTR at https://search.company.internal/search/ctr. If there are no impressions, the calculation will crash.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_ctr operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-ctr, Project: CTR-CALC-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 11:47:22 PST. While this might not happen often, if we try to calculate CTR when there are no impressions, the application will crash.

Our search analytics dashboard shows CTR metrics. If we look at a query with no impressions, the dashboard will crash.

We calculate CTR for thousands of queries. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

453.title: Search session timeout using multiplication instead of addition
453.description:
Critical issue with search session timeout calculations. I was trying to calculate session timeouts and the formula is using multiplication instead of addition.

When I use the check_session function, the timeout threshold is calculated as last activity time times timeout minutes. This should be last activity time plus timeout minutes.

I attempted to check session at https://search.company.internal/search/session. Sessions are timing out at completely wrong times.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, check_session operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-session, Project: SESSION-TIMEOUT-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 14:55:18 CET. The session timeout calculation is completely wrong. Sessions are timing out at astronomical timestamps.

Our search session management uses timeouts to expire inactive sessions. The wrong calculation is causing sessions to never timeout or timeout immediately.

We manage sessions for about 50,000 searches per day. The calculation should be last_activity + timedelta(minutes=timeout_minutes), not multiplication. This is a critical session management issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from multiplication to addition to properly calculate timeout thresholds.

455.title: Search index size calculation using exponentiation
455.description:
Critical issue with search index size calculations. I was trying to calculate index sizes and the formula is using exponentiation instead of multiplication.

When I use the calculate_index_size function, the size is calculated as document count to the power of average doc size. This gives completely wrong results.

I attempted to calculate index size at https://search.company.internal/search/index-size. The size estimates are astronomical and unusable.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_index_size operation. Production environment, Account: company-index-size, Project: SIZE-CALC-01.

Chrome 121 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:28:45 GMT. The index size calculation is completely wrong. Sizes should be in megabytes or gigabytes, but we're getting values in the trillions.

Our search index capacity planning uses size estimates. The wrong calculation is making all estimates meaningless.

We calculate index sizes for capacity planning. The calculation should be document_count * average_doc_size, not document_count ** average_doc_size. This is a critical capacity planning issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from exponentiation to multiplication to properly estimate index sizes.

457.title: Search query performance score using subtraction in wrong place
457.description:
Issue with search query performance score calculations. I was trying to calculate performance scores and the latency penalty is being added instead of subtracted.

When I use the calculate_performance function, the performance score is calculated as base score plus latency penalty. Latency should reduce performance scores, not increase them.

I attempted to calculate performance at https://search.company.internal/search/performance. Slow queries are getting higher performance scores instead of lower.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_performance operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-performance, Project: PERF-SCORE-01.

Firefox 121 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 12:42:18 EST. The performance score is backwards. Slow queries are being scored higher than fast queries.

Our search performance monitoring should penalize slow queries. The addition is causing the opposite effect.

We calculate performance scores for about 50,000 queries per day. The calculation should be base_score - latency_penalty, not plus. This is a performance monitoring issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to subtraction to properly penalize high latency.

459.title: Search A/B test sample size using division instead of multiplication
459.description:
Critical issue with search A/B test sample size calculations. I was trying to calculate sample sizes and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the calculate_sample_size function, the sample size is calculated as total users divided by test percentage. This should be multiplication to get the percentage of users.

I attempted to calculate sample size at https://search.company.internal/search/ab-test-sample. For 10% test, we're getting 10x too many users instead of 10% of users.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_sample_size operation. Production environment, Account: company-ab-test, Project: SAMPLE-SIZE-01.

Chrome 119 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 15:33:18 PST. The sample size calculation is completely wrong. We're including way too many users in A/B tests.

Our A/B testing system uses sample sizes to determine how many users to include. The wrong calculation is causing tests to include the wrong number of users.

We run about 10 A/B tests per month. The calculation should be total_users * (test_percentage / 100), not divided. This is a critical A/B testing issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly calculate sample sizes.

461.title: Search index optimization score canceling out fragmentation
461.description:
Issue with search index optimization score calculations. I was trying to calculate optimization scores and the fragmentation penalty is being added and subtracted, canceling out.

When I use the calculate_optimization function, the optimization score is calculated as base score minus fragmentation plus fragmentation. The fragmentation operations cancel out.

I attempted to calculate optimization at https://search.company.internal/search/optimize. The fragmentation isn't affecting the optimization score.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_optimization operation. Production environment, Account: company-index-optimize, Project: OPTIMIZE-SCORE-01.

Safari 17 on macOS 13.5, 2026-02-05 10:47:22 PST. The optimization scores are wrong because fragmentation isn't being considered. Highly fragmented indexes show the same score as optimized ones.

Our index optimization system should penalize fragmentation. The canceling operations are making fragmentation irrelevant.

We calculate optimization scores for index maintenance. The calculation should be base_score - fragmentation, not with the operations that cancel out. This is an index health monitoring issue.

The fragmentation operations need to be fixed to properly penalize fragmentation in optimization scores.

463.title: Search query complexity score using wrong weight
463.description:
Issue with search query complexity score calculations. I was trying to calculate complexity scores and the boolean operators weight is being multiplied by zero.

When I use the calculate_complexity function, the complexity score is calculated as term count plus filter count plus boolean operators times 0. The boolean operators don't contribute to complexity.

I attempted to calculate complexity at https://search.company.internal/search/complexity. Queries with many boolean operators show the same complexity as simple queries.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_complexity operation. Production environment, Account: company-query-complexity, Project: COMPLEXITY-SCORE-01.

Edge 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 13:55:18 CET. The complexity score isn't considering boolean operators. Complex boolean queries appear as simple as basic queries.

Our query complexity monitoring should account for boolean operators. The multiplication by zero is making them irrelevant.

We calculate complexity for about 50,000 queries per day. The calculation should include boolean_operators with a proper weight, not times 0. This is a complexity monitoring issue.

The multiplication by zero needs to be replaced with a proper weight factor for boolean operators.

465.title: Search result merging score using addition instead of averaging
465.description:
Critical issue with search result merging score calculations. I was trying to merge results from multiple sources and the scores are being added instead of averaged.

When I use the merge_results function, the merged score is calculated as sum of source scores. This should be average of source scores for proper score normalization.

I attempted to merge results at https://search.company.internal/search/merge. Results from multiple sources have inflated scores compared to single-source results.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, merge_results operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-merge, Project: MERGE-SCORE-01.

Chrome 120 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 11:28:45 GMT. The merged score calculation is wrong. Multi-source results are unfairly boosted compared to single-source results.

Our federated search merges results from multiple sources. The wrong scoring is biasing results toward items found in multiple sources.

We merge results for about 20,000 federated searches per day. The calculation should be sum(source_scores) / len(source_scores), not just sum. This is a search fairness issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from sum to average to properly normalize merged scores.

467.title: Search learning to rank feature weight using exponentiation
467.description:
Critical issue with search learning to rank feature weight calculations. I was trying to apply feature weights and the formula is using exponentiation instead of multiplication.

When I use the apply_ltr_weights function, the weighted feature is calculated as feature value to the power of weight. This gives completely wrong results.

I attempted to apply LTR weights at https://search.company.internal/search/ltr. The weighted features are astronomical or tiny depending on the values.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, apply_ltr_weights operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-ltr, Project: LTR-WEIGHT-01.

Firefox 121 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 14:42:18 EST. The learning to rank feature weighting is completely wrong. The exponentiation is making all weighted features meaningless.

Our search learning to rank system uses feature weights to combine signals. The wrong calculation is breaking the entire LTR model.

We apply LTR weights to about 40,000 searches per day. The calculation should be feature_value * weight, not feature_value ** weight. This is a critical search ranking issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from exponentiation to multiplication to properly weight LTR features.

469.title: Search query log sampling using wrong percentage
469.description:
Critical issue with search query log sampling. I was trying to sample query logs and the sampling is using division instead of multiplication for the percentage.

When I use the sample_queries function, the sample size is calculated as total queries divided by sample percentage. For 10% sampling, this gives 10x too many queries instead of 10% of queries.

I attempted to sample queries at https://search.company.internal/search/sample. The sample size is way too large.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, sample_queries operation. Production environment, Account: company-query-sample, Project: SAMPLE-SIZE-02.

Chrome 121 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 09:18:45 PST. The query sampling is including too many queries. We're sampling 10x more than intended.

Our query log analysis uses sampling to reduce processing load. The wrong calculation is causing us to process way more queries than needed.

We sample query logs for analysis. The calculation should be total_queries * (sample_percentage / 100), not divided. This is a resource efficiency issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly calculate sample sizes.

471.title: Search index shard allocation using subtraction instead of modulo
471.description:
Critical issue with search index shard allocation. I was trying to allocate documents to shards and the formula is using subtraction instead of modulo.

When I use the allocate_shard function, the shard ID is calculated as document ID minus number of shards. This doesn't distribute documents evenly across shards.

I attempted to allocate document at https://search.company.internal/search/allocate-shard. Documents are not being distributed evenly across shards.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, allocate_shard operation. Production environment, Account: company-shard-allocate, Project: SHARD-ALLOC-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 12:33:18 PST. The shard allocation is completely wrong. Documents are not evenly distributed, causing load imbalance.

Our search index sharding should distribute documents evenly. The wrong calculation is causing some shards to be overloaded while others are empty.

We allocate millions of documents to shards. The calculation should be document_id % num_shards, not document_id - num_shards. This is a critical load balancing issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to modulo to properly distribute documents across shards.

473.title: Search cache eviction score using addition instead of subtraction
473.description:
Critical issue with search cache eviction score calculations. I was trying to calculate eviction scores and the formula is using addition instead of subtraction.

When I use the calculate_eviction_score function, the eviction score is calculated as base score plus access frequency. High frequency items should have lower eviction scores (less likely to evict), not higher.

I attempted to calculate eviction score at https://search.company.internal/search/cache-evict. Frequently accessed items are being evicted first instead of last.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_eviction_score operation. Production environment, Account: company-cache-evict, Project: EVICT-SCORE-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 15:47:22 CET. The cache eviction is backwards. We're evicting the most useful cache entries and keeping the least useful ones.

Our search cache should evict least-recently-used or least-frequently-used items. The wrong calculation is causing us to evict the most valuable cache entries.

We manage cache eviction for search results. The calculation should be base_score - access_frequency, not plus. This is a critical cache efficiency issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to subtraction to properly score items for eviction.

475.title: Search query suggestion confidence divide by zero risk
475.description:
Critical issue with search query suggestion confidence calculations. I was trying to calculate suggestion confidence and there's a divide by zero risk.

When I use the calculate_suggestion_confidence function, the confidence is calculated as (suggestion_frequency / total_frequency) if total_frequency >= 0. The check should be > 0, not >= 0, because dividing by zero when total is 0 will cause a crash.

I attempted to calculate confidence at https://search.company.internal/search/suggestion-confidence. If there are no queries yet, the calculation will crash.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_suggestion_confidence operation. Production environment, Account: company-suggest-confidence, Project: CONFIDENCE-CALC-02.

Chrome 119 on Windows 10, timestamp 2026-02-05 10:33:18 GMT. While this might not happen often, if we try to calculate confidence when there are no queries, the application will crash.

Our query suggestion system shows confidence scores. If we encounter a new term with no query history, the system will crash.

We calculate suggestion confidence for thousands of terms. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

477.title: Search index merge priority using division instead of multiplication
477.description:
Critical issue with search index merge priority calculations. I was trying to calculate merge priorities and the formula is using division instead of multiplication.

When I use the calculate_merge_priority function, the priority is calculated as segment size divided by segment age. Older, larger segments should have higher priority, but the division gives the opposite.

I attempted to calculate merge priority at https://search.company.internal/search/merge-priority. Small, new segments are getting higher priority than large, old segments.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_merge_priority operation. Production environment, Account: company-index-merge, Project: MERGE-PRIORITY-01.

Firefox 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 13:28:45 EST. The merge priority is backwards. We're merging the wrong segments first.

Our index merge strategy should prioritize large, old segments. The wrong calculation is causing inefficient merge operations.

We calculate merge priorities for index maintenance. The calculation should be segment_size * segment_age, not divided. This is an index efficiency issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from division to multiplication to properly prioritize segments for merging.

When I use the apply_relevance_feedback function, the adjusted score is calculated as original score minus feedback weight times feedback value. Positive feedback should increase scores, not decrease them.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, apply_relevance_feedback operation. Production environment, Account: company-relevance-feedback, Project: FEEDBACK-WEIGHT-01.

Chrome 120 on macOS 14.1, timestamp 2026-02-05 11:47:22 PST. The relevance feedback is backwards. Items users like are being ranked lower.

Our search relevance feedback system should boost scores for items with positive feedback. The subtraction is causing the opposite effect.

We apply relevance feedback to about 25,000 searches per day. The calculation should be original_score + (feedback_weight * feedback_value), not minus. This is a critical search quality issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from subtraction to addition to properly apply positive feedback.

Our index replication monitoring uses lag to detect replication issues. The wrong calculation is making all lag measurements meaningless.

We monitor replication lag continuously. The calculation should be current_time - last_sync_time, not multiplication. This is a critical monitoring issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from multiplication to subtraction to properly calculate replication lag.

487.title: Search term suggestion edit distance using addition instead of minimum

We calculate edit distances for spell checking. The calculation should use min(substitution, insertion, deletion), not sum. This is a critical spell check issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from sum to minimum to properly calculate Levenshtein edit distance.

489.title: Search index warming progress divide by zero risk
489.description:

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_warming_progress operation. Production environment, Account: company-index-warming, Project: WARMING-PROGRESS-01.

Chrome 119 on macOS 14.2, timestamp 2026-02-05 10:47:22 PST. While this might not happen often, if we try to calculate progress for an empty index, the application will crash.

Our index warming monitoring shows progress percentages. If we encounter an empty index, the monitoring will crash.

We monitor index warming for new indexes. The divide by zero check should use > 0, not >= 0, to prevent crashes.

The comparison operator needs to be changed from >= to > to prevent potential division by zero errors.

491.title: Search query auto-correction threshold inverted
491.description:

When I use the auto_correct function, it checks if confidence < threshold to apply correction. This is inverted - correction should be applied when confidence >= threshold.

I attempted to auto-correct query at https://search.company.internal/search/auto-correct. High-confidence corrections are not being applied while low-confidence ones are.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, auto_correct operation. Production environment, Account: company-auto-correct, Project: CORRECT-THRESHOLD-01.

Safari 16 on macOS 13.6, 2026-02-05 13:55:18 PST. The auto-correction logic is completely backwards. We're applying uncertain corrections and skipping confident ones.

Our search auto-correction should apply corrections when we're confident. The inverted logic is causing poor corrections to be applied.

We auto-correct about 10,000 queries per day. The check should apply correction when confidence >= threshold, not <. This is a critical search quality issue.

The comparison logic needs to be inverted to correctly apply auto-corrections based on confidence threshold.

493.title: Search result freshness score using subtraction instead of division
493.description:
Critical issue with search result freshness score calculations. I was trying to calculate freshness scores and the formula is using subtraction instead of division.

When I use the calculate_freshness function, the freshness score is calculated as current time minus document time. This should be a ratio like 1 / (1 + age) for proper freshness scoring.

I attempted to calculate freshness at https://search.company.internal/search/freshness. The freshness scores are timestamps instead of normalized scores between 0 and 1.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_freshness operation. Production environment, Account: company-search-freshness, Project: FRESHNESS-SCORE-01.

Edge 119 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 11:28:45 CET. The freshness score calculation is wrong. Scores should be normalized between 0 and 1, but we're getting large timestamp differences.

Our search freshness boosting uses freshness scores to prefer recent content. The wrong calculation is making scores incomparable.

We calculate freshness for about 40,000 search results per day. The calculation should be 1 / (1 + age_in_days), not current_time - document_time. This is a search relevance issue.

The calculation needs to be changed to a proper freshness decay formula that produces normalized scores.

The calculation needs to be changed from exponentiation to multiplication to properly estimate backup sizes.

503.title: Search index health score canceling out error rate
503.description:

When I use the calculate_health_score function, the health score is calculated as base score minus error rate plus error rate. The error rate operations cancel out.

I attempted to calculate health score at https://search.company.internal/search/health-score. The error rate isn't affecting the health score.

This is in the Search Discovery Service, search module, calculate_health_score operation. Production environment, Account: company-index-health, Project: HEALTH-SCORE-01.

Edge 120 on Windows 11, 2026-02-05 10:33:18 CET. The health scores are wrong because error rates aren't being considered. Indexes with high error rates show the same health as healthy indexes.

Our index health monitoring should penalize high error rates. The canceling operations are making error rates irrelevant.

We calculate health scores for index monitoring. The calculation should be base_score - error_rate, not with the operations that cancel out. This is an index health monitoring issue.

The error rate operations need to be fixed to properly penalize errors in health scores.

We route millions of queries across our search cluster. The calculation should be query_hash % num_nodes, not query_hash + num_nodes. This is a critical load balancing issue.

The calculation needs to be changed from addition to modulo to properly distribute queries across nodes.
